agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order cancellation feature for an order management system. Three stakeholders (Product, Finance, Operations) have provided requirements that govern when and whether an order may be cancelled. The requirements create a logical impossibility when combined.","bounded_context":"Order Management / Order Lifecycle","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A customer's request to purchase goods, progressing through a lifecycle from placement to shipment."},{"term":"Cancellation","definition":"The act of voiding an order, preventing fulfillment and shipment."},{"term":"Shipment","definition":"The point at which goods leave the warehouse for delivery to the customer. Represents a physical, irreversible commitment of inventory."},{"term":"Invoice","definition":"A financial document recording the obligation to pay. Per Finance, invoicing is irreversible."},{"term":"Order Placement","definition":"The initial act of creating an order. Per Operations, this is the moment invoicing occurs."},{"term":"Invoice at Placement","definition":"Operations policy of generating an invoice at the moment an order is placed, to lock in pricing."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"An order can be cancelled at any time before it ships.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a product/business policy about the cancellation window. A VP could change the window (e.g., only within 30 minutes of placement). Not a data integrity constraint.","testable_assertion":"Given an order in any pre-shipment state, a cancellation request succeeds."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Orders that have been invoiced cannot be cancelled. Invoicing is irreversible.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Finance declares invoicing irreversible — this is a financial integrity constraint. Cancelling an invoiced order without a corresponding credit note or reversal would corrupt the financial ledger.","testable_assertion":"Given an order with an associated invoice, a cancellation request is rejected."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Orders are invoiced at the time of placement to lock in pricing.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an operational policy to prevent pricing drift. It could be changed to invoice at shipment or at a different lifecycle stage. It is a business decision, not a physical or data-integrity constraint.","testable_assertion":"Given a newly placed order, an invoice record exists immediately after placement."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Customer places an order and immediately requests cancellation","expected_behavior":"CONTRADICTED: BR-1 says allow it (not yet shipped), BR-2 + BR-3 say deny it (already invoiced at placement)."},{"scenario":"Order placed but warehouse has not begun picking","expected_behavior":"CONTRADICTED: Same conflict — order is invoiced at placement, so cancellation is blocked by finance rule, but permitted by product rule."},{"scenario":"Order placed, invoice generated, but shipment is days away","expected_behavior":"This is the entire pre-shipment window. Under current rules, every order in this window is simultaneously cancellable (BR-1) and non-cancellable (BR-2 + BR-3)."},{"scenario":"Partial shipment — some items shipped, some not","expected_behavior":"Even ignoring the core contradiction, partial shipment cancellation semantics are undefined."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"If we follow Finance (no cancellation post-invoice), customers can NEVER cancel, leading to chargebacks, refund disputes, and potential regulatory exposure. If we follow Product (allow cancellation pre-ship), we must reverse invoices, contradicting Finance's irreversibility claim and creating accounting reconciliation issues.","operational":"Operations invoices at placement for pricing lock-in. Any resolution that decouples invoicing from placement requires Operations to find an alternative mechanism (e.g., price reservations, quotes) to prevent pricing drift.","user":"Customers expect to cancel orders that haven't shipped. If the system silently prevents all cancellations due to immediate invoicing, customer satisfaction and trust will collapse. Support ticket volume will spike.","risk":"HIGH. Shipping any implementation without resolving this contradiction guarantees one stakeholder's requirement is violated. Choosing wrong could mean financial ledger corruption (if invoices are reversed) or customer harm (if cancellations are blocked).","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Cannot cancel any order under current combined rules, despite PM's stated intent. Will resort to chargebacks and support escalation."},{"group":"Finance Team","impact":"If invoices are reversed to allow cancellation, their irreversibility invariant is broken. Audit trail and ledger integrity are at risk."},{"group":"Operations Team","impact":"If invoicing is moved away from placement, they lose their pricing lock-in mechanism and must adopt an alternative."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will bear the brunt of customer frustration if cancellations are silently blocked. Need clear policy to communicate."},{"group":"Legal / Compliance","impact":"Consumer protection regulations in many jurisdictions require a cancellation window. A system that technically prevents all cancellations may create legal exposure."}]},"confidence":{"level":45,"reasoning":"The spec contains a direct logical contradiction across three stakeholder requirements. When combined, BR-1 (cancel before ship), BR-2 (cannot cancel if invoiced), and BR-3 (invoice at placement) produce an impossible state: orders are cancellable and non-cancellable simultaneously from the moment of placement until shipment. No acceptance criteria can be defined until stakeholders align. Confidence is below 50 due to this unresolved contradiction.","high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction is real and not a matter of interpretation","Each individual rule is internally coherent","The domain language (order, invoice, shipment, cancellation) is well-understood"],"low_confidence_areas":["Which stakeholder's rule should yield","Whether Finance means 'invoice' or actually means 'revenue recognition'","Whether Operations could accept a 'price lock' mechanism that is not a formal invoice","Whether a credit note or reversal process exists that Finance would accept as distinct from 'cancelling an invoice'"],"assumptions":["Invoice as used by Finance and Operations refers to the same concept","There is no existing credit note or invoice reversal process that Finance considers acceptable","Shipment is a discrete event, not a gradual process"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The three requirements are mutually exclusive. BR-3 (invoice at placement) + BR-2 (invoiced orders cannot be cancelled) = orders can never be cancelled. This directly contradicts BR-1 (orders can be cancelled before shipment). Every order enters an impossible state where it is simultaneously cancellable and non-cancellable from placement until shipment.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Management","Finance Team","Operations Team","Customers","Customer Support","Legal/Compliance"],"options":["A: Decouple pricing lock from invoicing. Operations uses a 'price reservation' or 'quote lock' at placement; invoicing moves to shipment. Cancellation is allowed pre-invoice (pre-ship). Finance and Product rules both hold.","B: Introduce a 'credit note' or 'invoice reversal' process. Orders are invoiced at placement, but cancellation triggers a credit note rather than deleting the invoice. Finance's ledger integrity is preserved (invoice still exists, offset by credit). Requires Finance to accept that 'irreversible' means the invoice record persists, not that the financial obligation persists.","C: Restrict cancellation window. PM accepts a narrower window (e.g., cancel within N minutes before invoicing occurs). Operations delays invoicing by N minutes. Compromise across all three teams.","D: PM requirement yields entirely. No cancellation after placement. Customers must go through a returns/refund process post-delivery instead."],"recommendation":"Option B is the most common industry pattern. Invoices are never deleted (preserving Finance's audit trail), but credit notes offset them, enabling cancellation. This preserves all three stakeholders' core intent: PM gets pre-ship cancellation, Finance gets immutable invoices, Operations gets pricing lock-in at placement. Recommend a stakeholder alignment meeting to confirm."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Cannot implement order cancellation as specified — the combined requirements create a logical impossibility where no order is ever cancellable despite an explicit cancellability requirement.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1 is negated by the conjunction of BR-2 and BR-3. All three cannot coexist without introducing a reconciliation mechanism (e.g., credit notes)."}]}
